claims.claim_text: In 2017, a GitLab engineer accidentally deleted a production database, and the recovery depended on the state of replicas and backups.
claims.span_hint: section: RAM: Your Counter Space, paragraph beginning 'In 2017, a GitLab engineer...'
claims.rationale: Allowlisted sources can verify the 2017 GitLab database deletion incident, but none on the allowlist directly support the specific postmortem-style claim about recovery depending on replicas and backups.

claims.claim_text: Thirty browser tabs alone can consume 4-6 GB of RAM.
claims.span_hint: section: RAM: Your Counter Space, blockquote beginning 'Pause and predict'
claims.rationale: Exact tab memory use varies too much by browser, site mix, extensions, and date, and no allowlisted source honestly backs this specific 4-6 GB figure.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes worker nodes can run either Linux or Microsoft Windows, while the control plane runs on Linux.
claims.span_hint: section: The Operating System: Your Restaurant Manager, paragraph beginning 'Here's the part that will matter...'
claims.rationale: The Kubernetes documentation directly states that the control plane runs on Linux and worker nodes can run either Windows or Linux.

claims.claim_text: AWS listed a Linux t2.large instance with 8 GiB of RAM in us-east-1 at $0.0928/hour on April 15, 2026.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Matters for Kubernetes, final paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a dated-specific price snapshot, and the allowlist does not provide a reliable archived April 15, 2026 page to prove that exact historical listing.

claims.claim_text: The Apollo Guidance Computer had 74 KB of memory, and a modern smartphone has millions of times more computing power than the computers used for the 1969 moon landing.
claims.rationale: Allowlisted sources support AGC word counts or roughly 72-76 KB depending on conversion, not this exact '74 KB' wording, and they do not honestly support the 'millions of times more' comparison as written.

claims.claim_text: Early computers used magnetic-core memory made of tiny rings, each ring stored one bit, and a full megabyte would require over 8 million rings.
claims.rationale: The source directly explains magnetic-core rings and one-bit-per-core storage, which supports the stated ring-count arithmetic for a megabyte.

claims.claim_text: SSDs have no moving parts and are generally faster, quieter, and more resistant to physical shock than hard disk drives.
claims.rationale: The source directly compares SSDs with HDDs on moving parts, speed, noise, and shock resistance.

claims.claim_text: In 1947, engineers working on the Harvard Mark II found a moth in the machine and logged it as an 'actual bug'.
claims.rationale: The Harvard Mark II article directly covers the 1947 moth incident and the logbook note about an actual bug.

further_reading.title: Windows containers in Kubernetes
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary Kubernetes documentation for the Linux control plane and mixed Linux/Windows worker-node model mentioned in the module.

further_reading.title: Amazon EC2 T2 Instances
further_reading.why_relevant: Concrete cloud instance page showing CPU, memory, region context, and pricing in one place.

further_reading.title: Apollo Guidance Computer
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful background for the module's comparison between modern devices and moon-era computing hardware.

notes: Per task rules, I excluded claims that appear only inside code blocks, tables, quiz items, or exercise steps. That means the StatCounter desktop-share line in the OS code block and the Common Mistakes table were intentionally not emitted here.
